content.title: Example 1 — Computer Assembly (Static Inner Builder)
content.body: The most common Java idiom. Builder is a static nested class; the Product's constructor is private. The product is immutable — all fields are `final`.

content.title: Computer — Static Inner Builder
Example 2 — SQL Query Builder
content.body: Demonstrates Builder for objects with collections and conditional logic. `where()` can be called multiple times. Conditional construction is impossible with standard constructors.

content.title: SQL Query Builder
Example 3 — Car vs. Manual Builder (Director Pattern)
content.body: One Director, two completely different products. `CarBuilder` produces a physical Car; `CarManualBuilder` produces a documentation Manual. They share no common interface — yet the Director uses both identically.

content.title: Car vs. Manual — Director Pattern
Example 4 — Document Generator (HTML/Markdown with Director)
content.body: Same Director method produces an HTML report or a Markdown report depending on which builder is passed. The Director's `constructStandardReport()` never changes.

content.title: Document Generator

flashcards.q: In the Car/Manual example, how does Builder guarantee the Manual matches the Car?
flashcards.a: Both CarBuilder and CarManualBuilder implement the same Builder interface with the same steps. The Director calls constructSportsCar() using both. Since the step sequence is identical, the Manual is structurally guaranteed to describe a sports car — they cannot drift out of sync.

flashcards.q: What is the Static Inner Builder idiom in Java and what problem does it solve?
flashcards.a: The Builder is a static nested class inside the Product class. The Product's constructor is private — only the inner Builder can call it. This prevents illegal construction outside the Builder and ensures the product is fully formed before exposure.

flashcards.q: What does method chaining (Fluent Interface) mean in the context of Builder?
flashcards.a: Each setter method returns `this` (the Builder instance), allowing calls to be chained: `builder.setA(x).setB(y).build()`. This makes the construction code read like a sentence and eliminates intermediate variable assignments.

problems.q: **Implement HttpRequest Builder (Medium):** Build an immutable `HttpRequest` class. Required fields: `url`, `method`. Optional: `body` (default null), `timeout` (default 30), `headers` (default empty). Use the Static Inner Builder idiom. The `build()` method should throw `IllegalArgumentException` if `url` is empty.
problems.hint: Required fields go in the Builder constructor. Optional fields get setters that return `this`. Validation logic belongs in `build()`, not in setters.
